title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement vincenzo.coia@gmail.com. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.1, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired [Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder][https://github.com/mozilla/inclusion]. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: Thank considering contributing discretes! document provides guidelines instructions contributing package.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: Please note project released Code Conduct. participating project agree abide terms.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: find bug, please open issue GitHub : clear, descriptive title Steps reproduce issue Expected behavior Actual behavior R session info (output sessionInfo()) minimal reproducible example (reprex) can create reprex using reprex package, example.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: Enhancement suggestions tracked GitHub issues. creating enhancement suggestion, please include: clear, descriptive title detailed description proposed enhancement Explanation enhancement useful Examples enhancement used suggestion adding new distribution family, please indicate think family significant enough included.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: welcome pull requests! ’s process: Fork repository clone locally. Create branch changes. Make changes following coding style guidelines. Write update tests cover changes. Run checks locally. Update documentation including roxygen2 comments vignettes needed. Commit changes clear, descriptive commit messages. Push fork submit pull request development branch.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: discretes follows tidyverse style guide. Key points: Use <- assignment, = Use snake_case function variable names Keep lines maximum 80 characters Add spaces around operators (x + y, x+y) Add spaces commas (f(x, y), f(x,y))

title: Contributing to discretes
text: exported functions must complete roxygen2 documentation Include @param parameters clear descriptions Include @returns describing function returns Include @examples working examples Use proper markdown formatting documentation

title: Contributing to discretes
text: questions contributing, please contact maintainer: Vincenzo Coia (vincenzo.coia@gmail.com).

title: Contributing to discretes
text: package
code: - in an initially stable state of development, with minor     subsequent development envisioned.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: directions required. Implement next_discrete() prev_discrete() every subclass. code prev_discrete() walking forward negated series, avoid infinite recursion. sequence owner already knows forward/backward steps behave, even unusual constructions Fibonacci-like streams concatenated series. **num_discretes(), has_discretes(), representative(), has_*_zero()** S3 methods also expected new series. Ensure coded avoid recursion loops.

title: Contributing to discretes
text: Infinity discrete values supported discretes package. Reasons : Inf -Inf naturally occurring members numeric vectors, encountered wild. closed set real numbers mathematically understood valid concept. day future, probability distributions containing infinity outcome may included probaverse, discretes package intended serve. Although complicated include, absence infinity outcome feels like noticeable hole package compromises quality, serving 90% use cases package. Notes: +0 -0 need tracked explicitly, since treated identical R. Behaviour around signed zero copy behaviour numeric vectors base R. allowing Inf -Inf numeric vectors “discretes” objects (like arithmetic series) causes inconsistencies: 1 / dsct_union(0, natural1()) give result 1 / natural0(), yet . Checking membership inverse checks membership 0 base series, since distinguishing +0 -0. numeric vectors allowed, clunky logic needed: ever numeric vector encountered, invert numeric vector check -Inf Inf. user inputs numeric vector act “discretes” object, ’s important process vector first, operations like unique(). One reason +0 -0: collapse one two unique(), inversion result one Inf -Inf rather .

title: Creating numeric series
text: Subsetting: dsct_keep() dsct_drop() restrict series outside interval. Combining: dsct_union() merges multiple series one.

title: Creating numeric series
text: Arithmetic — operations +, -, *, /, ^ supported one side number numeric series. example: Length-0 vectors allowed, , result empty series: Mathematical functions — exp() supported. non-negative series, log(), log2(), log10(), sqrt() also supported: functions may able applied well; use dsct_transform() (see ).
code: integers()^2 #> Loading required namespace: testthat #> Union series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... 2 * natural1() - 1   # odd positive integers #> Linear-transformed series of length Inf: #> 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, ... 1 / 2^integers()     # reciprocals of powers of 2 #> Reciprocal series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, ... numeric(0L) * natural1() #> Empty series. log(natural0()) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> -Inf, 0, 0.6931472, 1.098612, 1.386294, 1.609438, ...

title: Creating numeric series
text: transformations, use dsct_transform(). numeric vector, function applied directly: transforming object class "discretes" restrictions querying series can defined base series. function inverse must vectorized element-wise (cumsum()-style dependence elements). must supply domain range function. defaults real numbers. function must strictly monotonic given domain. example applying function pnorm(). range (0, 1), must given explicitly: function monotonic interval, restrict domain. example, cos() decreasing [0, pi] range [-1, 1], inverse acos(). Specify dir = "decreasing" indicate decreasing function.

title: Querying a numeric series
text: next_discrete() returns next n discrete values series starting reference point; prev_discrete() opposite direction. main way move along series without enumerating . discrete value, can include setting include_from = TRUE. default, next_discrete() prev_discrete() use include_from = FALSE, result starts strictly () . Traversal delegated underlying series, result mapped back.
code: x <- integers() next_discrete(x, from = 10) #> [1] 11 next_discrete(x, from = 10, n = 5) #> [1] 11 12 13 14 15 prev_discrete(x, from = 1.3, n = 5) #> [1]  1  0 -1 -2 -3

title: Querying a numeric series
text: Use has_discretes() check whether given values discrete values series. returns logical vector, one per queried value. NA queried values yields NA result.
code: has_discretes(natural1(), c(0, 1, 2, 2.5)) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE has_discretes(integers(), c(-10, 0, 10, NA)) #> [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE   NA

what: Getting discrete values: get_discretes_at() and get_discretes_in()
title: Querying a numeric series
text: get_discretes_at() — Grabs specified discrete values members series (within tol base series), corresponding discrete value series returned; otherwise dropped. result “canonical” discrete values positions. NA values kept place. get_discretes_in() — Returns discrete values within range. result ordered smallest largest. infinitely many discrete values range, get_discretes_in() throws error; use num_discretes() first check.
code: get_discretes_at(integers(), values = c(-10, 4, 3.5, 10, NA)) #> [1] -10   4  10  NA get_discretes_at(integers(), values = 5.5) #> integer(0) get_discretes_in(integers(), from = 6.6, to = 10.1) #> [1]  7  8  9 10 get_discretes_in(1 / arithmetic(1, 4, n_left = 3, n_right = 5)) #> Loading required namespace: testthat #> [1] -0.33333333 -0.14285714 -0.09090909  0.04761905  0.05882353  0.07692308 #> [7]  0.11111111  0.20000000  1.00000000

title: Querying a numeric series
text: num_discretes() returns many discrete values lie range. can return Inf infinite series.
code: num_discretes(integers(), from = -2, to = 5) #> [1] 8 num_discretes(1 / 2^integers(), from = 0, to = 1) #> [1] Inf

title: Querying a numeric series
text: sink limit point numeric series: discrete values get arbitrarily close , infinitely many discrete values near sink. sinks() lists sinks matrix locations directions: approached left (-1) right (+1). has_sink_in() has_sink_at() convenience functions test sink interval value. has_sink_at(), dir can "either" (direction), "left" "right" (sink approached side), "" (approached sides). series sink, “next” “previous” discrete value far side sink (e.g. next_discrete(reciprocals, = -1) returns nothing). Together, functions cover traversing, membership, extraction, counting, sink queries numeric series.
code: sinks(integers()) #>      location direction #> [1,]      Inf        -1 #> [2,]     -Inf         1 sinks(0.5^natural0()) #>      location direction #> [1,]        0         1 reciprocals <- 1 / integers() sinks(reciprocals) #>      location direction #> [1,]        0         1 #> [2,]        0        -1 has_sink_in(integers()) #> [1] TRUE has_sink_at(integers(), Inf) #> [1] TRUE has_sink_at(integers(), -Inf, dir = "right") #> [1] TRUE

title: Tolerance
text: Tolerance applied series see top level. passed nested transformed series used membership actually tested underlying series. two places used : Arithmetic progressions (arithmetic()): membership decided implied step index (x−representative)/spacing(x - representative) / spacing. floating point, index rarely exact integer (e.g. (0.3 - 0) / 0.1). value treated discrete value index within tol integer. Numeric-vector-based series (plain numeric vectors as_discretes()): membership decided testing whether queried value within tol stored discrete value. transformed series (e.g. 1 / arithmetic()), queried value mapped back underlying series logic applied tol; separate tolerance applied transformed values.

title: Tolerance
text: Without tolerance, values can hard recognize discrete values arithmetic series implied index might 2.999999999 instead 3.
code: x <- arithmetic(representative = 0, spacing = 0.1) has_discretes(x, 0.3) #> [1] TRUE has_discretes(x, 0.3, tol = 0) #> [1] FALSE

what: Example: tol with an explicit numeric series
title: Tolerance
text: Even series represented numeric vector, can still run tiny floating-point mismatches. want “mathematical series” behaviour explicit series, use tol = 0. want “numerical computing” behaviour (robust round-), keep tol positive.
code: v <- c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.1 * 3)   # last entry is not exactly 0.3  has_discretes(v, 0.3) #> [1] TRUE has_discretes(v, 0.3, tol = 0) #> [1] FALSE

title: Tolerance
text: numeric vector converted “discretes” object via as_discretes(), root numeric vector preserved, even series gets transformed. Consider transforming numeric vector vs. vector expressed “discretes” object. numeric vector gets transformed right away, whereas remains base series transformed “discretes” object. implications errors propagated differently. example, exponent slightly 1e-10 results significant error transformation may within default tol: means membership eagerly transformed numeric vector fails, vector preserved. tolerance applied root series.
code: raw <- 10^(-5:5) raw #>  [1] 1e-05 1e-04 1e-03 1e-02 1e-01 1e+00 1e+01 1e+02 1e+03 1e+04 1e+05 preserved <- 10^as_discretes(-5:5) preserved #> Transformed series of length 11: #> 1e-05, 1e-04, 0.001, ..., 1000, 10000, 1e+05 # Exponent slightly off from `5` q <- 10^(5 - 1e-10) # Error after transformation 10^5 - q #> [1] 2.302586e-05 has_discretes(raw, q) #> [1] FALSE has_discretes(preserved, q) #> [1] TRUE

title: Tolerance
text: Default usually fine: sqrt(.Machine$double.eps) intended smooth tiny floating-point noise. Use tol = 0 exactness: truly want explicit sets behave exactly, perhaps useful situations working ‘integer’ storage mode. Increase tol noisy values: values come computation measurement noticing unusual membership failures, increase tol accordingly.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Coia V (2026). discretes: Discrete Numeric Series. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/vincenzocoia/discretes.
code: @Manual{,   title = {discretes: Discrete Numeric Series},   author = {Vincenzo Coia},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/vincenzocoia/discretes}, }

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: discretes package provides framework representing numeric series may finite infinite. Think 1:Inf, without storing values explicitly. Series can traversed, tested membership, queried limit points (“sinks”). can manipulated create new series, transforming combining. name “discretes” reflects original use case representing support discrete probability distributions like Poisson Geometric, often infinite enumerable. package limited probability applications: designed general-purpose tool working enumerable numeric series.

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: Install discretes CRAN :
code: install.packages("discretes")

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: vectors R must finite, problem discretes. objects referred “numeric series”, class “discretes”. members referred “discrete values”. ’s next discrete value 10 series integers? previous five values 1.3? Test whether values discrete values series. Perform arithmetic operations series. new series created base series gets modified. See Creating numeric series vignette ’s allowed (e.g. monotonic transformations) use dsct_transform() custom transformations.
code: library(discretes) natural0() #> Integer series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ... next_discrete(integers(), from = 10) #> [1] 11 prev_discrete(integers(), from = 1.3, n = 5) #> [1]  1  0 -1 -2 -3 has_discretes(natural1(), c(0, 1)) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE 1 / 2^integers() #> Reciprocal series of length Inf: #> Loading required namespace: testthat #> ..., 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, ...

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: last series , 1 / 2^integers(), sink1 0 (approached right) sink infinity, best seen plotting:  Notice infinitely many discrete values close 0. thing “next” value looking left sink. can ask whether sink exists directly.
code: x <- 1 / 2^integers() plot(x) num_discretes(x, from = 0, to = 1) #> [1] Inf next_discrete(x, from = -1) #> numeric(0) has_sink_in(x, from = 0, to = 1) #> [1] TRUE has_sink_at(x, 0, dir = "right") #> [1] TRUE

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: two main vignettes explaining use package. Creating numeric series — Base series, arithmetic, custom manipulations. Querying numeric series — Traversing next_discrete/prev_discrete, membership has_discretes, extracting values get_discretes_at() / get_discretes_in(). addition, two technical vignettes interested details. Tolerance — tol used membership traversal. Signed zero — Behaviour +0 -0 numeric series.

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: series supported package include arithmetic series like integers, finite series numeric vector, series created (see Creating numeric series vignette). Specialized series like Fibonacci numbers explicitly supported. Dense countable sets like rational numbers also supported well-defined notion local successor/predecessor.

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: Please note discretes project released Contributor Code Conduct. contributing project, agree abide terms.

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: ‘Zseq’ provides access named integer sequences (e.g., Fibonacci numbers, prime numbers), provide general framework constructing transforming numeric series. ‘sets’ focuses finite set operations abstract set algebra, rather structured numeric series. ‘set6’ supported infinite sets via object-oriented abstractions, longer available CRAN. ‘peruse’ provides tools iterating general sequences, focus algebraic manipulation structured numeric series.

title: Discrete Numeric Series
text: Development package possible without funding support European Space Agency, BGC Engineering Inc., Politecnico di Milano. need package arose work probaverse project, aims provide tools probabilistic modeling inference R.

code: # S3 method for class 'discretes' Math(x, ...)

text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). ... Passed log() base argument op == "log".

title: Math group generic for numeric series — Math.discretes
code: exp(integers(from = 0, to = 3)) #> Transformed series of length 4: #> 1, 2.718282, 7.389056, 20.08554 log(natural1()) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> 0, 0.6931472, 1.098612, 1.386294, 1.609438, 1.791759, ... sqrt(integers(from = 0, to = 10)) #> Transformed series of length 11: #> 0, 1, 1.414214, ..., 2.828427, 3, 3.162278

code: # S3 method for class 'discretes' Ops(e1, e2)

text: e1, e2 Operands; one must numeric series (class discretes), single numeric.

text: numeric series resulting operation (e.g. series + number, number * series, series^number).

title: Arithmetic and power operators for numeric series — Ops.discretes
code: integers() + 1 #> Linear-transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, ... 2 * natural1() #> Linear-transformed series of length Inf: #> 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, ... 1 / integers(from = 1, to = 5) #> Reciprocal series of length 5: #> Loading required namespace: testthat #> 0.2, 0.25, 0.3333333, 0.5, 1 natural0()^2 #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...

title: Arithmetic series — arithmetic
text: Construct arithmetic progression, possibly infinite values. progression anchored representative extends n_left steps left (decreasing values) n_right steps right (increasing values) constant spacing consecutive terms.

title: Arithmetic series — arithmetic
code: arithmetic(representative, spacing, ..., n_left = Inf, n_right = Inf)

title: Arithmetic series — arithmetic
text: representative Numeric scalar giving known term progression. spacing Non-negative numeric scalar describing distance adjacent terms. ... Reserved future extensions; must empty. n_left, n_right Non-negative counts (possibly Inf, default) describing many steps exist left right representative.

title: Arithmetic series — arithmetic
text: numeric series (class dsct_arithmetic, inheriting discretes).

title: Arithmetic series — arithmetic
text: spacing can zero, results numeric series containing representative value single discrete value. series can contain -0 representative set .

title: Arithmetic series — arithmetic
code: arithmetic(representative = -0.6, spacing = 0.7) #> Arithmetic series of length Inf: #> ..., -2, -1.3, -0.6, 0.1, 0.8, 1.5, ... arithmetic(representative = 0.6, spacing = 0.7, n_right = 0) #> Arithmetic series of length Inf: #> ..., -2.9, -2.2, -1.5, -0.8, -0.1, 0.6 arithmetic(representative = 0, spacing = 2, n_left = 2, n_right = 2) #> Arithmetic series of length 5: #> -4, -2, 0, 2, 4  # Negative zero, resulting in `-Inf` upon inversion: has_negative_zero(arithmetic(-0, 1)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Convert a numeric series to a numeric vector — as.double.discretes
title: Convert a numeric series to a numeric vector — as.double.discretes
text: Return discrete values numeric series, finite. Throws error infinite.

title: Convert a numeric series to a numeric vector — as.double.discretes
code: # S3 method for class 'discretes' as.double(x, ...)

title: Convert a numeric series to a numeric vector — as.double.discretes
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). ... Arguments pass downstream .numeric() called resulting vector discrete values.

title: Convert a numeric series to a numeric vector — as.double.discretes
text: numeric vector containing discrete values x, ordered smallest largest. Returns numeric(0) interval contains discrete values. Numeric outputs wrapped .numeric().

what: Create a numeric series from a numeric vector — as_discretes
title: Create a numeric series from a numeric vector — as_discretes
text: Create numeric series numeric vector

title: Create a numeric series from a numeric vector — as_discretes
code: as_discretes(x)

title: Create a numeric series from a numeric vector — as_discretes
text: x numeric vector missing values. May contain infinity.

title: Create a numeric series from a numeric vector — as_discretes
text: numeric series (object class "discretes") whose discrete values unique values x.

title: Create a numeric series from a numeric vector — as_discretes
code: as_discretes(0:10) #> Numeric vector series of length 11: #> 0, 1, 2, ..., 8, 9, 10

text: R allow numbers hold integer type big. .integer() convert numbers NA, output always type "integer". as_integerish(), hand, rather keep original number change type "double" convert large numbers NA.

code: as_integerish(x)

text: x Numeric vector. specifically, atomic vector coercable numeric via .numeric() without becoming NA.
vector comprising whole numbers, type "integer" possible, least type "double".

.integer() converts number NA, as_integerish() instead drop fractional part number, keeping sign. Values x NA preserved NA. .integer() quite forgiving inputs. 'base' version 4.4.3, .integer("5.5") returns 5L, example. as_integerish() adopts behaviour allowing inputs get converted NA .numeric().

code: suppressWarnings(as.integer(1e100)) #> [1] NA discretes:::as_integerish(1e100) #> [1] 1e+100 discretes:::as_integerish(1e100 + 0.8) == 1e100 #> [1] TRUE discretes:::as_integerish(-1e100 - 0.8) == -1e100 #> [1] TRUE  x <- discretes:::as_integerish(c(NA, 1, -5.5)) is.integer(x) #> [1] TRUE  y <- discretes:::as_integerish(c(NA, 1, -5.5, 1e100)) is.integer(y) #> [1] FALSE

what: Linearly transform a numeric series — dsct_linear
title: Linearly transform a numeric series — dsct_linear
text: Apply linear function numeric series (m * x + b). Internal function; prefer using +, -, *, / instead.

title: Linearly transform a numeric series — dsct_linear
code: dsct_linear(x, m, b = NULL)

title: Linearly transform a numeric series — dsct_linear
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). m numeric value indicating multiplier. b numeric value indicating intercept. Allowed NULL (default) indicate transformation m * x without intercept, important distinguish signed zero.

title: Linearly transform a numeric series — dsct_linear
text: numeric series linear transformation applied.

title: Linearly transform a numeric series — dsct_linear
code: discretes:::dsct_linear(integers(), m = 2) #> Linear-transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., -4, -2, 0, 2, 4, 6, ... discretes:::dsct_linear(integers(), m = 2, b = 0.5) #> Linear-transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., -3.5, -1.5, 0.5, 2.5, 4.5, 6.5, ...

what: Raise a numeric series to a power — dsct_power
title: Raise a numeric series to a power — dsct_power
text: Apply power transformation numeric series x given power; , x^power. Internal; use ^ operator instead.

title: Raise a numeric series to a power — dsct_power
code: dsct_power(x, power)

title: Raise a numeric series to a power — dsct_power
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). power power raise series ; numeric length 0 1. x contains negative discrete values, power must integer, otherwise error thrown result contain complex numbers, supported.

title: Raise a numeric series to a power — dsct_power
text: numeric series whose discrete values result applying power transformation ^ discrete values x specified power.

title: Raise a numeric series to a power — dsct_power
code: ## These are the same discretes:::dsct_power(natural0(), power = 2) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... natural0()^2 #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...  ## These are also the same discretes:::dsct_power(integers(), power = 3) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., -8, -1, 0, 1, 8, 27, ... integers()^3 #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., -8, -1, 0, 1, 8, 27, ...

what: Exponentiation of a numeric series — dsct_raise
title: Exponentiation of a numeric series — dsct_raise
text: Exponentiate numeric series x given base; , base^x. dsct_exp() special case dsct_raise() base exp(1). Internal; use exp() ^ operators instead.

title: Exponentiation of a numeric series — dsct_raise
code: dsct_raise(x, base = exp(1))  dsct_exp(x)

title: Exponentiation of a numeric series — dsct_raise
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). base base use exponentiation; numeric length 0 1, negative values allowed (error thrown otherwise).

title: Exponentiation of a numeric series — dsct_raise
text: numeric series whose discrete values result exponentiating discrete value x base (, base^x).

title: Exponentiation of a numeric series — dsct_raise
code: # These are the same discretes:::dsct_exp(integers()) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.1353353, 0.3678794, 1, 2.718282, 7.389056, 20.08554, ... discretes:::dsct_raise(integers(), base = exp(1)) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.1353353, 0.3678794, 1, 2.718282, 7.389056, 20.08554, ... exp(integers()) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.1353353, 0.3678794, 1, 2.718282, 7.389056, 20.08554, ...  # These are also the same discretes:::dsct_raise(integers(), base = 2) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, ... 2^integers() #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, ...  # This also works. Notice how the set reduces. 0^integers() #> Numeric vector series of length 3: #> 0, 1, Inf

what: Extract discrete values from a numeric series — get_discretes_at

text: Extract finite subset discrete values numeric series asking specific values (get_discretes_at()) setting range (get_discretes_in()). get_discretes_at(), value values tested membership using tol (passed underlying series); member, corresponding discrete value returned, otherwise dropped. NA values kept place.

See next_discrete() details. , Reference values, possibly infinite. must less equal ; must length-1 numeric vectors. include_from, include_to value included query? value? must length-1 logical vectors.

Membership tested tol underlying series, returning discrete values series rather supplied values. Discrete values within tol supplied values dropped. error thrown get_discretes_in() infinitely many points range.

what: Test for sinks in a numeric series — has_sink_in
title: Test for sinks in a numeric series — has_sink_in
text: has_sink_in() tests whether numeric series sink interval [, ]. has_sink_at() tests whether sink given value, optionally specific direction.

title: Test for sinks in a numeric series — has_sink_in
code: has_sink_in(x, from = -Inf, to = Inf)  has_sink_at(x, value, dir = c("either", "left", "right", "both"))

title: Test for sinks in a numeric series — has_sink_in
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). , Reference values, possibly infinite. must less equal ; must length-1 numeric vectors. value Single numeric check existence sink. dir Single character: direction sink. "either" (default) ignores direction; "left" "right" require sink approached side; "" requires approached sides.

title: Test for sinks in a numeric series — has_sink_in
text: length-one logical: TRUE sink exists range (has_sink_in) value given dir (has_sink_at); FALSE otherwise.

title: Test for sinks in a numeric series — has_sink_in
code: # The set of integers have sinks at +Inf and -Inf has_sink_in(integers()) #> [1] TRUE has_sink_at(integers(), Inf) #> [1] TRUE has_sink_at(integers(), -Inf, dir = "right") #> [1] TRUE  # The set 1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ... has a sink at 0 approached from the right. halves <- 0.5^natural0() has_sink_in(halves, to = 0) #> [1] FALSE has_sink_at(halves, 0, dir = "right") #> [1] TRUE  # Reciprocal of integers: sink at 0 from both sides reciprocals <- 1 / integers() has_sink_at(reciprocals, 0, dir = "both") #> [1] TRUE

title: Integer numeric series — integers
text: Use integers() create numeric series whose discrete values integers within specified range, possibly unbounded either end. Use natural0() natural1() natural numbers starting 0 1.

title: Integer numeric series — integers
code: integers(from = -Inf, to = Inf)  natural1()  natural0()

title: Integer numeric series — integers
text: , Numeric values defining range integers. Defaults -Inf Inf, representing integers; series closed, -Inf Inf never discrete values.

title: Integer numeric series — integers
text: numeric series (arithmetic, class "dsct_arithmetic") whose discrete values integers specified range.

title: Integer numeric series — integers
code: integers()                  # All integers #> Integer series of length Inf: #> ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ... integers(from = 0)          # Non-negative integers #> Integer series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ... integers(to = 1.5)          # Ends at 1. #> Integer series of length Inf: #> ..., -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1 integers(-5, 5) # Integers from -5 to 5. #> Integer series of length 11: #> -5, -4, -3, ..., 3, 4, 5 natural1() #> Integer series of length Inf: #> 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, ... natural0() #> Integer series of length Inf: #> 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ...  # Infinity is never contained in the series. has_discretes(integers(), Inf) #> [1] FALSE

title: Check if values are between two bounds — is_between

title: Check if values are between two bounds — is_between
code: discretes:::is_between(1:5, lower = 2, upper = 2) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE discretes:::is_between(1:5, lower = 2, upper = 2, include_lower = FALSE) #> [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE discretes:::is_between(1:5, lower = 2, upper = 4, include_upper = FALSE) #> [1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE

title: Logarithm of a numeric series — dsct_log
code: ## These are the same discretes:::dsct_log(natural0(), base = exp(1)) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> -Inf, 0, 0.6931472, 1.098612, 1.386294, 1.609438, ... log(natural0()) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> -Inf, 0, 0.6931472, 1.098612, 1.386294, 1.609438, ...  ## These are also the same discretes:::dsct_log(natural0(), base = 10) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> -Inf, 0, 0.30103, 0.4771213, 0.60206, 0.69897, ... log(natural0(), base = 10) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> -Inf, 0, 0.30103, 0.4771213, 0.60206, 0.69897, ... log10(natural0()) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> -Inf, 0, 0.30103, 0.4771213, 0.60206, 0.69897, ...

what: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
title: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
text: Check numeric series contains zero negative sign (-0) positive sign (+0). See details.

title: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
code: has_negative_zero(x)  has_positive_zero(x)

title: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes").

title: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
text: single logical: whether -0 discrete value series has_negative_zero(), whether +0 discrete value has_positive_zero(). can TRUE; see details.

title: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
text: +0 -0 identical R, latent sign appears reciprocals: 1 / +0 Inf, 1 / -0 -Inf. has_negative_zero() has_positive_zero() functions report whether -0 +0 discrete values numeric series. Behaviour consistent signed zero numeric vectors. numeric series can contain -0 +0, like c(0, -0). one zero returned next_discrete(), prev_discrete(), get_discretes_in(), unique(c(0, -0)). presence remains latent appears series inverted, giving Inf -Inf. See examples.

title: Check if a numeric series has a signed zero — has_negative_zero
code: has_negative_zero(integers()) #> [1] FALSE has_positive_zero(integers()) #> [1] TRUE  # Integer 0 can never be negative, but double can: has_negative_zero(-integers()) #> [1] FALSE has_negative_zero(-1.5 * integers()) #> [1] FALSE  # -0 and +0 can co-exist, but are never double counted. However, they # get expressed differently when the series is inverted. a <- c(0, -0) num_discretes(a) #> [1] 1 num_discretes(1 / a) #> [1] 2  b <- dsct_union(integers(from = -1, to = 1), -0) num_discretes(b) #> [1] 3 num_discretes(1 / b) #> [1] 4

title: Traversing a numeric series — next_discrete
text: next_discrete() prev_discrete() find n discrete values numeric series next reference point. num_discretes() finds number discrete values within range.

title: Traversing a numeric series — next_discrete
code: next_discrete(   x,   from,   ...,   n = 1L,   include_from = FALSE,   tol = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps) )  prev_discrete(   x,   from,   ...,   n = 1L,   include_from = FALSE,   tol = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps) )

title: Traversing a numeric series — next_discrete
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). Reference value start searching ; single numeric. ... Reserved future extensions; must empty. n Number discrete values find; single positive integer. include_from value included query? Single logical; defaults TRUE. tol Numerical tolerance testing membership. Single non-negative numeric. See next_discrete() details.

title: Traversing a numeric series — next_discrete
text: vector sequential points starting , included vector discrete value numeric series include_from = TRUE. length vector n, return empty numeric vector discrete value. next_discrete() increasing, prev_discrete() decreasing, earlier values encountered sooner.

title: Traversing a numeric series — next_discrete
text: transformed combined series (e.g. 1 / integers()), traversal delegated base series, tol passed calls. means tol realized underlying series: Numeric vector (as_discretes(...)): value member within tol stored value. Arithmetic series: implied step index (distance representative steps) treated integer within tol integer. See Tolerance vignette examples.

title: Traversing a numeric series — next_discrete
code: x <- integers(from = 2) next_discrete(x, from = 1.3) #> [1] 2 prev_discrete(x, from = 4, n = 10) #> [1] 3 2 next_discrete(x - 0.7, from = 1.3, n = 4) #> [1] 2.3 3.3 4.3 5.3

title: Number of discrete values in a series — num_discretes
code: num_discretes(-3:3) #> [1] 7 num_discretes(c(0.4, 0.4, 0.4, 0)) #> [1] 2  x <- arithmetic(-3.2, spacing = 0.5) num_discretes(x) #> [1] Inf num_discretes(x, from = -2, to = 2) #> [1] 8 num_discretes(1 / x, from = -2, to = 2) #> [1] Inf

title: Plot a numeric series — plot.discretes
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). , Numeric values defining range plot; single numerics. ... Additional arguments passed underlying plot() function. closeness Numeric value indicating close (non-infinite) sinks points longer plotted. infinite number points around sink. tol Passed dsct_keep() subsetting series .

title: Plot a numeric series — plot.discretes
text: Invisibly returns input x object printing plot Base R.

title: Plot a numeric series — plot.discretes
text: Sinks finite values indicated vertical dotted gray lines. red tick mark used indicate finite sink value part series. series extends infinity either direction, three arrows (< >) drawn indicate . infinity part series, last arrow red. simple plotting scheme naive handling infinite discrete values: closeness parameter adjust scale data, may require tuning often user. series extends infinity (either direction), arbitrary cutoff 10 units beyond last finite sink representative() value (whichever closer infinite sink) used. can manually adjusted changing parameters.

title: Plot a numeric series — plot.discretes
text: function takes long time plot, likely series slowly varying behaviour next sink, like 1 / natural1(), discrete values pile rapidly approaching sink slowly. avoid many points plotted, increase closeness argument.

title: Plot a numeric series — plot.discretes
code: plot(integers())  plot(integers(), from = -50, to = 50)  plot(0.5^natural1(), closeness = 1e-3)  plot(dsct_union(0.5^natural1(), 0), closeness = 1e-3)

title: Print a numeric series — print.discretes
code: print(integers()) #> Integer series of length Inf: #> ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ... print(1 / natural1()) #> Reciprocal series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.1666667, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3333333, 0.5, 1 print(-1 / natural1()) #> Negated series of length Inf: #> -1, -0.5, -0.3333333, -0.25, -0.2, -0.1666667, ... print(1 / integers()) #> Reciprocal series of length Inf: #> -1, -0.5, -0.3333333, ..., 0.5, 1, Inf print(1 / integers(), len = 1) #> Reciprocal series of length Inf: #> ..., Inf print(1 / integers(), len = 0) #> Reciprocal series of length Inf.

title: Sinks — sinks
text: Sinks limit points numeric series. means discrete values get arbitrarily close sink (left right), infinitely many discrete values around sink. function returns matrix sinks numeric series.

title: Sinks — sinks
code: sinks(x)

title: Sinks — sinks
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes").

title: Sinks — sinks
text: matrix two columns: location direction. row corresponds sink, location location sink (possibly infinite), direction indicates whether sink approached left (-1) right (1).

title: Sinks — sinks
code: # The set of integers have sinks at +Inf and -Inf sinks(integers()) #>      location direction #> [1,]      Inf        -1 #> [2,]     -Inf         1  # The set 1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ... has a sink at 0 approached from the right. halves <- 0.5^natural0() sinks(halves) #>      location direction #> [1,]        0         1  # The reciprocal of the integers has a sink at 0 approached from both the # left and right; while the integer 0 gets mapped to Inf, infinity is not a # sink because discrete values don't get arbitrarily close to it. reciprocals <- 1 / integers() sinks(reciprocals) #>      location direction #> [1,]        0         1 #> [2,]        0        -1 has_discretes(reciprocals, Inf) # Yet Inf is a discrete value. #> [1] TRUE

title: Subset a numeric series — dsct_drop
code: dsct_drop(   x,   from = -Inf,   to = Inf,   ...,   include_from = TRUE,   include_to = TRUE )  dsct_keep(   x,   from = -Inf,   to = Inf,   ...,   include_from = TRUE,   include_to = TRUE )

title: Subset a numeric series — dsct_drop
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). , Numeric values defining range keep; single numerics <= . ... Reserved future extensions; must empty. include_from, include_to Logical values indicating whether values included kept range; single logicals.

title: Subset a numeric series — dsct_drop
text: numeric series representing subset discrete values within specified range.

title: Subset a numeric series — dsct_drop
code: x <- integers(from = -3) dsct_keep(x, from = -1.5, to = 2.5) #> Subset series of length 4: #> -1, 0, 1, 2 dsct_keep(x, to = 2) #> Subset series of length 6: #> -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2

what: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
title: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
text: Apply function strictly increasing strictly decreasing numeric series.

title: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
code: dsct_transform(x, fun, ...)  # S3 method for class 'discretes' dsct_transform(   x,   fun,   inv,   ...,   domain = c(-Inf, Inf),   range = c(-Inf, Inf),   dir = c("increasing", "decreasing") )

title: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes"). fun, inv vectorized, strictly monotonic function apply discrete values, inverse, inv. ... Arguments pass specific methods. domain, range Numeric vectors length 2, indicating domain range fun (, interval fun valid, interval fun maps). dir string, either "increasing" "decreasing", indicating monotonicity function fun.

title: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
text: numeric series transformation applied.

title: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
text: Strictly increasing means x1 < x2, holds fun(x1) < fun(x2), values real line. function -1/x, example, strictly increasing: derivative increasing, switches smaller values x = 0, therefore strictly increasing. Strictly decreasing opposite, fun(x1) > fun(x2). decreasing function provided, transformation negated internally first, transformed fun(-x).

title: Monotonically transform a numeric series — dsct_transform
code: dsct_transform(integers(), fun = pnorm, inv = qnorm, range = c(0, 1)) #> Transformed series of length Inf: #> ..., 0.02275013, 0.1586553, 0.5, 0.8413447, 0.9772499, 0.9986501, ... dsct_transform(   as_discretes(0:3),   fun = cos,   inv = acos,   domain = c(0, pi),   range = c(-1, 1),   dir = "decreasing" ) #> Transformed series of length 4: #> -0.9899925, -0.4161468, 0.5403023, 1  # For numeric inputs, function is applied directly. # Other arguments beyond `fun` get absorbed in `...` and are not used. dsct_transform(0:5, exp) #> [1]   1.000000   2.718282   7.389056  20.085537  54.598150 148.413159 dsct_transform(0:5, exp, log) #> [1]   1.000000   2.718282   7.389056  20.085537  54.598150 148.413159

title: Construct vector of zeroes — zeroes_vector
text: Make numeric (double) vector zeroes length 2: contains +0 series contains +0, contains -0 series contains -0. Internal; useful delegating behaviour signed zero R numeric vectors.

title: Construct vector of zeroes — zeroes_vector
code: zeroes_vector(x)

title: Construct vector of zeroes — zeroes_vector
text: x Numeric series (numeric vector object class "discretes").

title: Construct vector of zeroes — zeroes_vector
text: numeric (double) vector zeroes, either length 0, 1, 2.

title: Construct vector of zeroes — zeroes_vector
code: (none <- discretes:::zeroes_vector(natural1())) #> numeric(0) 1 / none #> numeric(0)  (pos <- discretes:::zeroes_vector(integers())) #> [1] 0 1 / pos #> [1] Inf  (neg <- discretes:::zeroes_vector(arithmetic(-0, 1))) #> [1] 0 1 / neg #> [1] -Inf  (posneg <- discretes:::zeroes_vector(dsct_union(integers(), -0))) #> [1] 0 0 1 / posneg #> [1] -Inf  Inf
